source: Sellbom, M., Wygant, D. B., Tarescavage, A. M., & Ben-Porath, Y. S. (2022). Using the MMPI-3 in Forensic Psychological Assessments. In C. Garofalo & J. J. Sijtsema (Eds.), Clinical Forensic Psychology. Springer.

scale_categories.description: Measure various forms of response styles that, when excessively present, could invalidate an MMPI-3 test protocol or indicate the need to qualify the interpretation.

subcategories.description: Unintentional or intentional responding that manifests as unscorable, random/inconsistent/careless, or indiscriminant fixed (acquiescent or counter-acquiescent) responding.

scales.description: Combination of random and fixed indiscriminant responding
Inconsistent responding
Indiscriminant fixed responding

subcategories.description: Measure intentional or unintentional exaggeration or fabrication of psychopathology symptoms as well as non-credible somatic and cognitive complaints.

scales.description: Over-reporting validity scale based on rare responses in the general population
Over-reporting validity scale based on rare responses in general and psychiatric populations
Over-reporting validity scale based on rare somatic complaints in general and medical patient populations
Over-reporting validity scale based on non-credible somatic and cognitive complaints
Over-reporting validity scale associated with failure on performance validity tests

subcategories.description: Assess overly virtuous responding (or positive impression management) and a possibly non-credible avowal of good psychological adjustment and resilience.

scales.description: Under-reporting validity scale based on rarely claimed moral attributes or activities
Under-reporting validity scale describing an avowal of good psychological adjustment

scale_categories.description: Operationalize broadband psychopathology spectra of internalizing, thought disorder, and externalizing. They map onto broader level dimensions identified in a wide range of psychopathology research. At the top of the interpretive hierarchy, they reflect general and pervasive dysfunction in their respective areas.

scales.description: Pervasive problems associated with mood and affect
Pervasive problems with disordered thinking
Pervasive problems with under-controlled or acting out behavior

scale_categories.description: Slightly revised and shortened versions of their MMPI-2 and MMPI-2-RF counterparts. Unlike the original MMPI/MMPI-2 Clinical Scales, the RC Scales reflect transdiagnostic, dimensional psychological constructs rather than psychiatric syndromes.

scales.description: General emotional distress; unhappiness and dissatisfaction
Pre-occupation with various types of health complaints
Attenuated positive emotional experiences; significant anhedonia; disengagement
Antisocial proclivities, impulsivity, recklessness, and irresponsible behavior
Self-referential beliefs that others pose a threat; persecutory delusions
Dysfunctional anxiety, anger, irritability, fear, guilt
Unusual thoughts or perceptions; dissociation
Excessive activation, drive, aggression, and grandiosity

scale_categories.description: The most narrowband symptom and trait measures on the instrument, organized into four thematic domains.

scales.description: Overall feeling of physical debilitation and poor health
Complaints about faintness, weakness, bodily sensations, loss of balance, etc.
Difficulties with restrictive eating, binge-eating, and purging behaviors
Complaints about memory and difficulties concentrating
Suicidal ideation and reports of suicide attempts; preoccupation with death
Belief that problems cannot be solved or goals be reached
Lacking in confidence, feelings of worthlessness
Perception that one is inefficacious; indecisiveness
Poor stress tolerance; nervousness; difficulty with time pressure
Excessive worry and preoccupation; anxious apprehension
Engagement in compulsive or ritualistic behaviors
Intense anxiety; frights; panic; frequent nightmares
Poor anger control; frustration intolerance; impatient with others
Fears that considerably inhibit everyday activities
Problematic family relationships; familial alienation
Significant problems at school and at home; stealing as a youth
Abuse of alcohol and drugs currently and/or in the past
Poor impulse control and lack of planfulness
Increased excitation and energy level; euphoria; racing thoughts
Aggressive behavior of both physical and verbal nature
Non self-referential beliefs expressing a generally low opinion and mistrust of others
Ideation regarding special qualities and abilities
Being domineering and assertive in relationships with others
Misanthropic attitudes about people; disliking being around others
Eschewing or not deriving pleasure from social events
Prone to feel inhibited and anxious around others; bashful

scale_categories.description: Revised versions of their MMPI-2 and MMPI-2-RF counterparts. Represent dimensional personality traits with an abnormal range. They correspond well to a dimensional alternative to the categorical personality disorder framework and align well with the trait domains included in the Alternative Model for Personality Disorders (AMPD) in Section III of the DSM-5.

scales.description: Proactive, goal-directed aggression; assertiveness; grandiosity
Proclivities toward poor reality testing
Disinhibited behavior; impulsivity; sensation seeking; risk taking
Temperamental proclivity for anxiety, insecurity, worry, anger, and fear
Temperamental proclivity for anhedonia and social disengagement
